answer: When concatenating string literals, Python automatically joins them if they are next to each other. However, you cannot concatenate a variable and a string literal without using the + operator; attempting to do so results in a SyntaxError: invalid syntax.

Out-of-range indices used for indexing in Python strings result in an error (IndexError), while out-of-range indices used for slicing are handled gracefully. The error raised for out-of-range indexing is IndexError.
Four numeric types are explicitly mentioned in the document: int, float, Decimal, Fraction.

When you assign a list to another variable and then modify the original list, both variables refer to the same list and changes are seen through both. To create a separate copy of the list and avoid this behavior, use slicing (e.g., list[:] ).
